content:
Title says all. The upshot is that we'd get SAFETY comments per operation. Thoughts?

https://rust-lang.github.io/rust-clippy/master/index.html#multiple_unsafe_ops_per_block

I don't think the lint is useful in all cases. Some examples:

// SAFETY: The caller just gave us ownership of these fields.
// INVARIANT: A linked list with one item should be cyclic.
unsafe {
    (*item).next = item;
    (*item).prev = item;
}

// SAFETY: `ptr` is valid by the safety requirements of this function. And we own a
// reference count via `task_get_pid_ns()`.
// CAST: `Self` is a `repr(transparent)` wrapper around `bindings::pid_namespace`.
Some(unsafe { ARef::from_raw(ptr::NonNull::new_unchecked(ptr.cast::<PidNamespace>())) })
Must it be useful in all cases to be useful globally?
I think it's best to avoid lints with false positives.

It's not really a false positive, though. The safety comment says "these fields", so the lint is correct but the code wants to cover both with a single comment. The latter case already has two comments in a single block.
By false positive I mean that I don't think the code needs to be changed.

Sure. If we dereference, then the earlier sentence is

I think it's best to avoid lints where I don't think the code needs to be changed.

Which doesn't sound quite as persuasive. This is semantics of course, but it matters if we want to debate in good faith.
do you think we need to change the code in linked list?
I'd probably put an allow on the first example, and I'd change the second one to two blocks.
so does that count as an improvement on the code because of the lint from your PoV?
For the unchanged instance, no. For the changed one, yes

Can you help me understand why we're trying to decide if this is useful or not based on one specific case, rather than the aggregate? In other words, what would have to be true for the benefit to outweigh the cost (of having to change or allow this case)?

First, the particular cases are counter cases, i.e. they provide the information about the "cost" we are going to pay for the proposal.

Second, given it's your proposal, I think it's your job to provide the aggregate? Do you have data to prove that the benefit outweighs the cost?

Last, I don't think it's in a decision-making stage, Alice was simple providing some counter examples. No one has yet said anything decisive.

Could you help me understand the rush?

No rush! I'm happy to prove it out.

I think it's best to avoid lints with false positives.

This sounded pretty decisive to me, which is why we're having this debate
It has a "I think" in it ;-) That's to me a sign of personal perspective.
Heh, OK. I'll try it and see.
Appreciate your effort of looking into this (despite I don't quite like this lint either, but I could be wrong ;-))

The main difficulty is that addr_of{,mut}! is very often used with a raw pointer, so you end up writing

// SAFETY: `ptr` is valid because ...
let field = unsafe { addr_of_mut!((*ptr).field) };
// SAFETY: `field` is valid because `ptr` is valid.
unsafe { use_field(field) };
Ah yes. The addr_of one is another good case where following the lint makes code worse.
Probably more common than the ones I shared
Assuming field projection allows us to project NonNull<T> to NonNull<T::field> safely, we can revisit this when that's a thing.
Naively I would expect that to remain unsafe so that it can use pointer::add over wrapping_add. But I don't know.

This means that addr_of_mut!((*ptr).field) still requires the projection to field to be in-bounds, using the same rules as offset

Yeah, you're right - it would still be unsafe.
